coredata.dc:title: 3-Dimensional ordered reduced graphene oxide embedded with N-doped graphene quantum dots for high performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Bui, Thi Ai Ngoc
Nguyen, Thuy Giang
Darmanto, Win
Doong, Ruey-An
coredata.dc:description: The development of electrode materials with high energy and power densities is timely needed. In this study, the 0-dimensional N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) embedded into 3-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide (N-GQD/3DrGO) hybrid materials were hydrothermally fabricated for high performance supercapacitor application. The 2 – 6 nm N-GQDs with an average particle size of 4 ± 0.5 nm are homogenously dispersed onto the porous 3DrGO, resulting in the increase in the specific surface area with continuous meso‑macroporous channels. Moreover, the bimodal pore size distribution with a suitable meso‑macropore size range of 2.1 – 77.2 nm accelerates the electron and ion transports inside N-GQD/3DrGO to improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor application. N-GQD/3DrGO exhibits a superior specific capacitance of 361 F g − 1 at 2 A g − 1 in the presence of the voltage range of -1 – 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl) using Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. In addition, N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials show excellent long-term cycling stability, and 89.2% of the initial capacitance can be retained after 10,000 cycles. The embedded N-GQDs can minimize the re-stacking of rGO nanosheets as well as improve the pore texture and conductivity, while the 3D rGO serves as a carbon backbone to reduce the diffusion path of electrons and ions. This advantage results in a superior energy density of 111.2 − 24.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 260 − 5000 W kg−1. The excellent electrochemical performance clearly demonstrates that combination of different morphologies of graphene-based nanomaterials is a novel strategy to fabricate a superior electrode material for supercapacitor applications, which can provide a unique alternative to explore the new boundary of energy-related nanomaterials for high performance asymmetric energy storage devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-GQD/3DrGO nanocomposites as the electrode materials for high performance symmetric supercapacitor application.

(a) SEM image of 3D rGO, (b) HR-TEM image, (c) histogram, and (d) AFM image of N-GQDs, (e) TEM, and (f) HR-TEM images of N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials. Insets of figure (a) and (b) are the 3D morphology of 3D rGO and d-spacing of the N-GQDs, respectively.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, and (c) FTIR spectra of N-GQDs, 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials, (d) Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), (e) BET surface area, and (f) pore size distribution of 3D rGO based materials.
XPS (a) survey scan spectra of rGO, N-GQDs and N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials and deconvoluted (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, and (d) N 1 s peaks of N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials.

Cyclic voltammogram (CV) curves of (a) 3D rGO and (b) N-GQD/3DrGO at scan rates of 5 – 100 mV s − 1, (c) specific capacitance of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO as a function of scan rate, (d) galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of N-GQD/3DrGO at current densities of 2 – 15 A g − 1, (e) specific capacitance of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO as a function of current density, and (f) Nyquist plot of the 3D rGO based nanomaterials.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram (CV) and (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of 3D rGO based materials at 100 mV s − 1 and 0.5 A g − 1, respectively, (c) Ragone plots of N-GQD/3DrGO with various N-GQDs ratios, and (d) cycling stability of N-GQD/3DrGO nanocomposite. Insets of Figure d is the charge/discharge curve of the N-GQD/3DrGO in the potential range from −1 to 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at 2 A g − 1.

head.ce:title: 3-Dimensional ordered reduced graphene oxide embedded with N-doped graphene quantum dots for high performance supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of electrode materials with high energy and power densities is timely needed. In this study, the 0-dimensional N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) embedded into 3-dimensional porous reduced graphene oxide (N-GQD/3DrGO) hybrid materials were hydrothermally fabricated for high performance supercapacitor application. The 2 – 6 nm N-GQDs with an average particle size of 4 ± 0.5 nm are homogenously dispersed onto the porous 3DrGO, resulting in the increase in the specific surface area with continuous meso‑macroporous channels. Moreover, the bimodal pore size distribution with a suitable meso‑macropore size range of 2.1 – 77.2 nm accelerates the electron and ion transports inside N-GQD/3DrGO to improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor application. N-GQD/3DrGO exhibits a superior specific capacitance of 361 F g − 1 at 2 A g − 1 in the presence of the voltage range of -1 – 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl) using Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. In addition, N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials show excellent long-term cycling stability, and 89.2% of the initial capacitance can be retained after 10,000 cycles. The embedded N-GQDs can minimize the re-stacking of rGO nanosheets as well as improve the pore texture and conductivity, while the 3D rGO serves as a carbon backbone to reduce the diffusion path of electrons and ions. This advantage results in a superior energy density of 111.2 − 24.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 260 − 5000 W kg−1. The excellent electrochemical performance clearly demonstrates that combination of different morphologies of graphene-based nanomaterials is a novel strategy to fabricate a superior electrode material for supercapacitor applications, which can provide a unique alternative to explore the new boundary of energy-related nanomaterials for high performance asymmetric energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: The boost of economy and technology in different branches has raised the demand for highly efficient energy storage devices. An urgent need has been dedicated to the efforts of developing new and novel electrode materials for enhancing the electrochemical performance of electron storage devices [1]. Supercapacitor is of great interest in modern technology and industry because of its excellent portability, high power density, short charging/discharging time, and long lifespan [2,3]. Therefore, the search for outstanding electrode materials with excellent power and energy densities is highly demanded [4-6].

Graphene-based nanomaterials with various morphologies and pore textures including 0-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), 2D reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and 3D rGO have been widely employed as effective materials for energy storage [7-9]. Of those materials used, 3D rGO has received considerable attention because the 3D morphology has the advantage of preventing self-restacking, and subsequently results in featuring a relatively high porosity and easily accessible pore texture for the diffusion of electrons and electrolyte ions [10-12]. Moreover, the large surface area and highly porous structure make 3D rGO an excellent electrode material to access electron transport, resulting in the enhancement of electrochemical performance [11]. Dong et al. [13] have prepared a 3D graphene foam-cobalt oxide nanocomposites as a supercapacitor electrode, and the specific capacitance could be up to 1100 F g − 1 at 10 A g − 1 [13]. Moreover, the fabrication of 3D Fe3O4/rGO nanocomposite greatly improved the specific capacitance from 317 F g − 1 at 27 mV s − 1 to 455 F g − 1 at 8 mV s − 1 of a hybrid supercapacitor [14]. Usually, the 3D structured porous carbon materials exhibit the advantages of large pore volume, high surface area, and continuous pore texture, which can shorten the electron and ion diffusion paths, thereby enhancing the electrochemical performance of electrostatic double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) [13].

In addition to 3D rGO, GQDs have been regarded as a newly explored member of the graphene family for various applications including biosensing, drug delivery, photovoltaics, and energy conversion and storage [15-17]. A previous study prepared the 1D GQD-doped polyaniline nanocomposites, and a specific capacitance of 1044 F g − 1 at 1 A g − 1 was reported [15,18,19]. Moreover, GQDs can be surface-modified by either doping with heteroatoms (e.g. N, B, P, and S) or creating a high number of edge states to improve their supercapacitive performance [20-22]. Among the heteroatoms used, N is one of the most often employed elements because of its good electron donor property to provide more active sites for reaction. N atoms can attract more protons to intrinsically improve the overall conductivity and EDLC behavior of GQDs, and thereby lead to the generation of a pseudo-capacitance activity [20,23]. Therefore, addition of N-GQDs into 3D rGO electrode materials can create a high degree of edge defects for active sites to enhance the electrochemical activities. However, the doping of N-GQDs into the 3D rGO structure for the enhancement of EDLC performance has received less attention.

Herein, the N-doped GQDs embedded into 3D rGO nanostructures (N-GQD/3DrGO) were hydrothermally fabricated to serve as an electrode material for high performance supercapacitor application. As shown in Scheme 1 , the 3D rGO was fabricated by hydrothermal route at 150 °C for 9 h in the presence of l-ascorbic acid, and then N-GQDs, prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and urea, were embedded into the meso‑macroporous structures of 3D rGO. The morphology and microstructure including crystallinity, specific surface area, and chemical species of highly porous N-GQD/3DrGO, where 3D rGO serves as the conductive backbone materials and the homogeneously dispersed N-GQDs can increase the conductivity and active sites, were identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, surface area analysis, and X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS). In addition, the electrochemical experiments including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed to understand the electron transport behaviors as well as to calculate the superior specific capacitance in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Moreover, Ragone plot was used to compare the energy density and mechanical stability of N-GQD/3DrGO with the other reported results. Results clearly indicate that the combination of 0D N-GQD and 3D rGO can constitute a promising graphene-based electrode material with superior supercapacitive performance, which can provide an alternative to design the novel carbonaceous materials with various dimensions for a wide variety of electrochemical application such as supercapacitors, batteries, and hydrogen evolution from water.

Graphite powder, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), and phosphoric acid were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and disodium hydrogen phosphate anhydrous were purchased from Merck Co. Hydrogen peroxide and urea were the products of Riedel-de Haën. Sodium hydroxide was obtained from J. T. Baker, and l-ascorbic acid was purchased from Lancaster. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received. Moreover, bi-distilled deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare all the solutions unless otherwise mentioned.

Graphene oxide (GO) was first synthesized through a thermal method by adding 2.0 g of graphite powder into a solution containing concentrated sulfuric and phosphorus acids under stirring at 50 °C [24,25]. A 6-g potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was slowly added into the graphite solution at 50 °C for 10 h under well-mixing conditions. The mixture was then carefully poured into DI water in an ice bath, and 5 mL of H2O2 was added into the solution. After the change in solution color from brown to yellow, 1 N HCl was added to remove Mn2+ ions first and then neutralized with phosphate buffered saline at pH 11. The GO was obtained by washing again with DI water and ethanol to remove the residual acid and impurities.

The preparation of 3D rGO was achieved by mixing 150 mL of GO solution at 1.5 mg mL−1 with 0.9 g of l-ascorbic acid for 30 min under vigorous mixing conditions. This mixture was then poured into a 200-mL Teflon-lined autoclave vessel and hydrothermally treated at 150 °C for 9 h. After cooled to room temperature, the 3D structured rGO product was freeze-dried for 24 h, and then stored at room temperature before use.

The N-doped GQDs were hydrothermally fabricated according to our previous study with minor modifications [26]. In brief, the same amount (0.23 g) of citric acid and urea were dissolved in 5 mL of DI water under stirring conditions, and then transferred into a 30-mL Teflon lined autoclave tube. After hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 4 h, the N-GQDs solution was harvested by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The final product was suspended in DI water again and dialyzed with 1000-Da dialysis bag with DI water as the dialysate for 24 h to remove those un-reacted raw materials and impurities. The obtained N-GQDs were then stored at room temperature before use.

The N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials at 10 wt% N-GQDs were fabricated using a hydrothermal treatment. Briefly, 100 mg 3D rGO was soaked into 20 mL of N-GQDs solution at 0.5 mg mL−1 for 24 hrs, and then transferred the mixture into an autoclave tube. After hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 hrs, the final product was freeze-dried and stored at room temperature before use.

The surface morphology and particle size of 3D rGO based carbonaceous materials were characterized by a JEOL transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-ARM 200F) and a high-resolution-TEM (HRTEM) (JEM-2010) operated at 15 and 200 kV, respectively. The topography as well as the thickness of N-GQDs was characterized by an atomic force microscope (AFM) in tapping mode (Dimension Edge, Bruker Co.). The structure of 3D rGO based materials was examined with a Hitachi SU8010 scanning electron microscope (SEM) at 15 kV. The Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) surface area and pore texture of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO were determined by Micrometrics ASAP 2020 analyzer. The crystallinity was identified with a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å) at 10 – 70° 2θ. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (FT-720, Horiba, Kyoto, Japan) was used to record the functional groups of the materials. Raman spectra were obtained using a Bruker Senterra Raman microscope. The Ulvac-PHI 1600X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) equipped with Al Kα radiation source (1486.6 ± 0.2 eV) was used to characterize the chemical species of N-GQD/3DrGO based nanomaterials. The thermal property of carbonaceous nanomaterials was recorded with a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Mettler Toledo) in a temperature range of 30 – 800 °C ramped at 10 °C min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere.

The Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT 302 N electrochemical test system was used to analyze the CV and GCD of N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials in a three-electrode cell using 1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared by mixing 95 wt% N-GQD/3DrGO and 5 wt% PVDF in NMP. This mixture was well-mixed into a homogeneous paste, placed onto 1 × 1 cm2 nickel foam, and then dried overnight at 60 °C under vacuum. Moreover, Ag/AgCl and platinum wire were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The CV curves were recorded in a voltage range of −1 – 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at various scan rates of 5 – 100 mV s − 1, and the GCD performance was also determined at current densities of 2 – 15 A g − 1.

For supercapacitor application, the two-electrode system was fabricated. In addition, three different N-GQDs-to-3D rGO ratios at 5, 10 and 20 wt% were used as the working electrodes to evaluate the capacitive performance of N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials. These working electrodes were denoted as N-GQD/3DrGO-x, where x is the mass ratio of N-GQDs in N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials. Also, the specific capacitance of N-GQD/3DrGO can thus be calculated:

The electron microscopes including SEM, TEM, and HRTEM were first used to identify the morphology and particle size distribution of the N-GQD/3DrGO based nanomaterials. As displayed in Fig. 1 a, the rGO nanosheets are edge-interconnected to each other to form the highly porous architecture of 3D rGO, which possess a large number of macropores. The inset of Fig. 1a clearly shows the 3D morphology, signifying the successful fabrication of 3D rGO material. For N-GQDs, the HRTEM image exhibits a well and homogenous distribution (Fig. 1b), and the fringes distance of 0.21 nm of single nanoparticle lattice is observed (inset of Fig. 1b and Fig. S1, Supplementary data), which is ascribed to the (100) plane of graphene [26,27]. This means that the as-prepared N-GQDs have a good crystalline structure. The histogram in Fig. 1c indicates that the particle sizes of N-GQDs are 2 – 6 nm with an average diameter of 4 ± 0.5 nm, which is corroborated with the previously reported data [26]. The AFM image displays a uniform distribution of N-GQDs with its height of −0.5 to 1.0 nm (0.8 nm on average), suggesting that the N-GQD is a 2 – 5 layered carbonaceous material (Fig. 1d). Furthermore, the TEM image shows a homogeneous distribution of 10 wt% N-GQDs onto the 3D rGO surface (Fig. 1e). Since the particle size of N-GQDs onto 3D rGO is too small, the HRTEM is further used to characterize the existence of N-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1f, the HRTEM image of N-GQD/3DrGO shows a uniform distribution of 2 – 6 nm N-GQDs onto 3D rGO structures, which is consistent with the as-prepared N-GQDs. In addition, a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm is observed, which is the (002) plane of 3D rGO. These results strongly indicate the successful embedment of N-GQDs into highly porous 3D rGO structures. The intercalated N-GQDs can serve as the spacer to minimize the re-stacking of rGO [28,29], resulting in the cross-interconnection of rGO to form 3D structure of N-GQD/rGO.

The microstructural properties of 3D rGO based nanomaterials were further characterized. As shown in Fig. 2 a, a small and broad peak centered at 2θ of 28.98° with d spacing of 0.34 nm [30], is observed in the XRD patterns of N-GQDs, which is the characteristic peak of graphite oxide [31]. The peak of 3D rGO appearing at 25° 2θ is typically a diffraction peak of the (002) plane of graphene [32]. and then shifts slightly to 26.05° 2θ when 10 wt% N-GQDs are added to the 3D rGO structures. This result indicates the successful embedment of N-GQDs into the 3D structure of rGO after hydrothermal reaction [33]. The corresponding interlayer spacing of N-GQD/3DrGO is calculated as 0.356 nm, which is slightly larger than that of 3D rGO (0.343 nm). This enlarged interlayer spacing means the formation of more graphitic structures in N-GQD/3DrGO when N-GQDs are embedded into the graphene sheets of 3D structures [30,34].

The degree of graphitic structure in carbon materials was further illustrated by Raman spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the Raman spectrum of N-GQDs exhibits peaks at 1369 and 1589 cm–1, which are the D band from the sp3 defects and the G band of the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbons, respectively [35]. Moreover, both the 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO spectra exhibit the prominent D and G peaks, and the ID/IG ratio increases from 1.05 for N-GQDs to 1.12 for 3D rGO and 1.16 for N-GQD/3DrGO. It is noteworthy that N element contains a large number of edge states, and can be easily reacted with oxygen-containing groups in a carbon-based matrix of rGO [36]. Therefore, the high ID/IG ratio of N-GQD/3DrGO in comparison with pure N-GQDs and 3D rGO is mainly due to the increase in the defect to disorder the rGO structures after doping N-GQDs into the 3D rGO.

Fig. 2c displays the FTIR spectra of N-GQD/3DrGO based nanomaterials. The peak at 3425 cm–1 in N-GQDs is the stretching vibration of –OH/– NH group. The characteristic peak near 1637 cm−1 is the typical stretching of the carbonyl group (C = O) in N-GQDs structure [26,37]. The N-GQD/3DrGO also shows similar functional groups with N-GQDs, highlighting the successful embedment of N-GQDs into 3D rGO and the hydrophilic nature of N-GQD/3DrGO. The band at 1560 cm–1 in 3D rGO based nanomaterials is the bending of C = C stretching, indicating the stability of graphene backbone [37]. Moreover, the small peak at 1383 cm–1 is the absorption of O − H/C − O − C in all the as-prepared materials [38,39]. Compared to 3D rGO, the peak at 1383 cm–1 slightly shifts to a lower wavelength in N-GQD/3DrGO, presumably attributed to the presence of C–N stretching from N-GQDs which can be proven from the peak at 1057 cm–1 (C − N in-plane bending vibration) appearing in both N-GQDs and N-GQD/3DrGO [40].

The thermal properties of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials were further evaluated by TGA from 30 to 800 °C. As described in Fig. 2d, both the TGA curves of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO follow similar patterns. The weight loss at 30 – 150 °C in the thermograms of pure 3D rGO is the evaporation of water molecules. The further increase in temperature to 200 – 300 °C rapidly oxidizes rGO to CO2 and/or the decomposition of some thermal-labile organic groups [14]. The significant weight loss at 500 – 600 °C is mainly due to the exothermal oxidation of oxygen-containing functional groups and the decomposition of graphitic backbone [25]. Moreover, the remaining percentage of 3D rGO is 40.3 wt%, and only 19.8 wt% remains for N-GQD/3DrGO after the thermal treatment at 600 °C.

The specific surface area and pore texture of 3D rGO based materials were further determined. As displayed in Fig. 2e, both the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO exhibit the type IV isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop in the relative pressure (P/Po) range of 0.45 – 0.95, which represents a capillary condensation in the region of mesopores and macropores [25]. The mesopore is mainly from the N-GQDs or rGO nanosheets, while the macropore is generated inside the 3D structure the edge-inter-connected of 2D rGO to each other. The specific surface area of 3D rGO is 190.2 m2 g − 1, and then increases to 230.6 m2 g − 1 when 10 wt% N-GQDs are added to the 3D rGO. The enhanced specific surface area of N-GQD/3DrGO is mainly due to the incorporation of quantum sized N-GQDs into a 3D rGO structure. Interestingly, the pore size distribution of pure 3D rGO is bimodal, which locates in the range of 1.8 – 4.1 and 4.1 – 86.8 nm with a mean pore size of 18.7 nm. After the embedment of N-GQDs, the N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterial also shows the bimodal patterns but decreases obviously to 2.1 – 3.1 and 3.1 − 77.2 nm (Fig. 2f), which clearly confirms that 2 – 6 nm N-GQDs can be successfully embedded into the meso‑macropores of 3D rGO network to increase the specific surface area [41].

The chemical species of elements in N-GQD/3DrGO were further examined by XPS. As clearly displayed in Fig. 3 a, the survey scan of 3D rGO shows the typical C 1 s (283.2 eV) and O 1 s (531.5 eV) peaks. The high peak intensity of C 1 s (86.18%) in comparison with O 1 s peak (13.82%) highlights the fact that GO is thermally reduced to rGO. Moreover, the N 1 s peak is clearly observed in both N-GQDs and N-GQD/3DrGO. The N 1 s peak slightly shifts from 399.3 eV for N-GQDs to 398.3 eV for N-GQD/3DrGO, indicating the production of more reduced species of N elements in N-GQDs after doping into 3D rGO.

Fig. 3b shows the deconvoluted C 1 s peak of N-GQD/3DrGO. Four major peaks at 283.12, 283.83, 284.93, and 286.73 eV are clearly observed in the deconvoluted C 1 s peak. Peaks at 283.12 and 286.73 eV are the aromatic C = C and carboxylic HO−C=O groups, respectively, while another two peaks belong to C − N (283.83 eV) and C − O (284.93 eV) [42,43], which confirm the successful doping of nitrogen element into GQDs. Furthermore, the deconvoluted O 1 s peak shows the occurrence of carbonyl (C=O) (529.88 eV), ether (C − O) (531.77 eV), and phenol (C−OH) (533.09 eV) groups (Fig. 3c) [23]. It is interesting to note that the chemical species of deconvoluted C 1 s and O1s peaks of N-GQD/3DrGO are similar to those deconvoluted peaks of 3D rGO in Fig. S2 (Supplementary data), which prove the carbon backbone function of 3D rGO and the good wettability of the N-GQD/3DrGO for electrochemical experiment. For N 1 s deconvolution, the N-GQD/3DrGO displays the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N peaks at 398.27 and 401.09 eV, respectively (Fig. 3d). It is noteworthy that the deconvoluted results of O 1 s and N 1 s indicate the existence of hydrophilic functional groups in the N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials, which may feature the pseudo-capacitive behavior, particularly in the presence of aqueous electrolytes, to enhance the electrochemical performance of N-GQD/3DrGO [23,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of n-gqd/3drgo based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The CV curves of pure 3D rGO based nanomaterials were investigated using the three-electrode system over the voltage range of −1 – 1 V with 1 M Na2SO4 as the neutral electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CV curves of 3D rGO generally exhibit the quasi-rectangular shapes at scan rates of 5 – 100 mV s − 1, elaborating the outstanding capacitive behavior of 3D rGO. Moreover, the CV curve still maintains its quasi-rectangular shape as the scan rate increases to 100 mV s − 1, which signifies the excellent rate capability of 3D rGO. After the addition of 10 wt% N-GQDs into 3D rGO structures, the CV curves still retain an ideal rectangular voltammogram with no distorted shape at all the scan rates of 5 – 100 mV s − 1 (Fig. 4b), indicating a relatively low resistance of electron transport throughout electrode material and a remarkably excellent electrochemical reversibility for capacitive behavior. It is noteworthy that the N-GQD/3DrGO curves exhibit a redox pair at −0.5/0.25 V (vs Ag/AgCl), presumably contributed from the reversible Faradaic reaction of pyrrolic-N/pyridine-N from N-GQDs [21]. This redox pair definitely results in an additional increase in pseudo-capacitance [21,23]. In particular, the voltammetric current density curve of N-GQD/3DrGO is much significantly larger than that of pure 3D rGO (Fig. S3, Supplementary data), which depicts a high specific capacitance of N-GQD/3DrGO due to the contribution of N-GQDs.

Fig. 4c illustrates the specific capacitance of 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO as a function of the scan rate. The specific capacitance of pure 3D rGO is 216 F g − 1 at 5 mV s − 1 and then decreases slightly upon the increasing scan rate. A 57% of the initial capacitance is retained at 100 mV s − 1 for pure 3D rGO. Interestingly, the specific capacitance of N-GQD/3DrGO is 302 F g − 1 at 5 mV s − 1, which corresponds to the increase in 140% compared to that of pure 3D rGO. The embedment of quantum-sized N-GQDs produces abundant edge sites to minimize the restacking of 2D rGO nanosheets, leading to the enhancement of the electrochemical activity and the increase in conductivity for supercapacitive performance. Moreover, the specific capacitance of N-GQD/3DrGO can retain 50% at 100 mV s − 1, presumably because the interconnected meso‑macroporous structures of 3D rGO can shorten the diffusion length of electrons and electrolytes, and thereby retain the specific capacitance at high scan rates.

Fig. 4d displays the GCD curves of N-GQD/3DrGO in the presence of 1 M Na2SO4 solution at current densities of 2 – 15 A g − 1. The GCD curves of N-GQD/3DrGO electrodes exhibit a symmetrical triangular shape, indicating the outstanding reversible characteristics and the excellent carrier transport within electrode materials. Moreover, the IR drop of N-GQD/3DrGO, the initial voltage loss from the discharge curve, is hardly observed. Different from N-GQD/3DrGO, a small IR drop of 0.05 V at 15 A g − 1 is observed in the GCD curves of pure 3D rGO (Fig. S3b, Supplementary data). These results signify that the addition of N-GQDs accelerates the diffusion of ions and reduces the internal resistance of rGO as a result of the increased hydrophilic polarity of N-GQDs [19]. Notably, the discharge time of N-GQD/3DrGO (360 s) is also longer in comparison with the pure 3D rGO (170 s), depicting the excellence in supercapacitive performance of hybrid nanomaterials. The GCD curves of pure 3D rGO at different current densities of 2 – 15 A g–1 is observed in Fig. S3c (Supplementary data).

Fig. 4e shows the specific capacitance of the graphene-based nanomaterials as a function of current density ranging from 2 to 15 A g − 1. The pure 3D rGO electrode shows the capacitance of 224.5 F g − 1 at 2 A g − 1, which is higher than those reported 2D rGO nanosheets [45,46]. The N-GQD/3DrGO electrode material exhibits an excellent capacitance at 361 F g − 1 at 2 A g − 1, which corresponds to 160% higher than that of 3D rGO. This result signifies the key role of N-GQDs in the enhanced electrochemical performance. Similar to the CV curves, the specific capacitance of 3D rGO based nanomaterials decreases gradually as the current density increases, and the specific capacitances of 192 and 125 F g − 1 are observed for N-GQD/3DrGO and pure 3D rGO, respectively, at 15 A g − 1. The low capacitance at high current density is mainly due to the increase in IR drop when the current density is high, which is in good agreement with the reported results [47,48]. At high current density, the diffusion of electrolyte ions is likely resistant and keeps them away from the pores of the electrode materials, resulting in the restriction of charge storage. Moreover, the low specific capacitance of 3D rGO in comparison with N-GQD/3DrGO is presumably attributed to the pore texture and specific surface area. Several studies have indicated that the high specific surface area and mesopore at 3 – 7 nm play pivot roles in energy storage [49,50]. The addition of N-GQDs can not only increase the surface area of the electrode material, but also shifts the pore size distribution to mesoporous region, resulting in the enhanced electrochemical performance on energy storage. These results convincingly depict that the existence of N-GQDs substantially improves the conductivity of N-GQD/3DrGO nanomaterials as well as performs a good rate capability.

The EIS analysis was performed to further understand the interfacial kinetics of 3D rGO based nanomaterials (Fig. 4f). The Nyquist plot displays a traditional EIS curve which contains a semicircle follows by a vertically straight line. In addition, the intercept on the real Z’ axis is the equivalent series resistance (Rs) of electrode materials. The Rs value is 2.4 Ω for pure 3D rGO and 3.6 Ω when 10 wt% N-GQDs are added into 3D rGO, showing that the electrolyte transport resistance of both 3D rGO and N-GQD/3DrGO are low. The radius of the semicircle indicates the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which is a function of the diffusion of electrolyte ions at the interface between 3D rGO based materials and Na2SO4 electrolyte [19]. After adding N-GQDs, the Rct decreases from 76 Ω for pure 3D rGO to 52 Ω for N-GQD/3DrGO. The decrease in resistance in the presence of N-GQD is mainly due to the fact that N-GQDs are conductively 0-dimensional carbon materials, and the homogeneous dispersion of N-GQDs onto rGO obviously increases the conductivity and the charge transfer rate of N-GQD/3DrGO. Furthermore, the straight line of N-GQD/3DrGO is more vertical in comparison with the pure 3D rGO. These results indicate that the ion diffusion resistance of the as-prepared novel hybrid materials is relatively low, and can be an ideal capacitive material for supercapacitor applications [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performances of N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials with different mass loadings of N-GQDs were further examined using two-electrode system. Three different mass loadings from 5 to 20 wt% N-GQDs were added into the 3D rGO support. Fig. 5 a compares the CV curves of various N-GQD/3DrGO electrode materials at 100 mV s − 1 in the potential window of −1 to 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl). All the N-GQD/3DrGO materials exhibit the rectangular shapes, indicating the EDLC behavior of the N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials. In addition, the electrochemical performance of N-GQD/3DrGO follows the order N-GQD/3DrGO-10 > N-GQD/3DrGO-5 > N-GQD/3DrGO-20. Similar to the CV curve, the symmetrically triangular shapes of GCD curves at 0.5 A g − 1 also evidently feature the superior supercapacitive performance of N-GQD/3DrGO materials with relatively low internal resistance. (Fig. 5b). It is worth noting that the GCD curve of N-GQD/3DrGO-10 electrode is 825 s, which is in particular much longer than those of the other two devices with different mass loadings of N-GQDs, which accounts only for 600 s and 490 s for N-GQD/3DrGO-5 and N-GQD/3DrGO-20, respectively. This result indicates that the mass loading of 10 wt% N-GQDs can benefit the N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials because of the attribution of a large number of active sites of N-GQDs to improve ion diffusion, thereby enhancing the surface accessibility for N-GQD/3DrGO [21,23]. Since N-GQDs have been reported as the effective electrode materials for energy storage [49,50], the increase in N-GQD loading from 5 to 10 wt% can enhance the electrochemical performance of N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid materials because of the increase in conductivity and edged active sites [51]. However, an excessive N-GQDs loading at 20 wt% would cause the aggregation of N-GQDs onto rGO, resulting in the decrease in charge-charge time of GCD curve.

The Ragone plot is an important indicator to verify the electrochemically capacitive performance of electrode materials. Fig. 5c displays the Ragone plot of N-GQD/3DrGO-10 nanomaterials based on the GCD resulting from the two electrodes assembly in the potential window of 2 V using 1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. The N-GQD/3DrGO-10 exhibits superior energy and power densities of 111.2 Wh kg−1 and 260 W kg−1 at 0.5 A g − 1, respectively. Correspondingly, the supercapacitor remains the high energy density of 24.2 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 5000 W kg−1 at 8 A g − 1. This symmetric electrochemical performance of N-GQD/3DrGO is superior to those of reported results using various metal oxide/carbon nanocomposites and 3D rGO nanomaterials as the electrode materials [52-56]. Qin et al. [53] fabricated 3DGF@PPy@MnO2 for symmetric supercapacitor application, and found that the energy and power densities were in the range of 28.0 – 14.0 Wh kg−1 and 500 – 13,000 W kg−1, respectively. Wang et al. [56] used PG@Mn3O4||AG electrode for asymmetric supercapacitor. The PG@Mn3O4||AG electrodes exhibits 30.1 – 15.8 Wh kg−1 at 475 – 9500 W kg−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 [56]. In this study, the excellent electrochemical performance of N-GQD/3DrGO is attributed to the fact that the suitable meso‑macroporous channels and the embedment of conductive N-GQDs shorten the ion diffusion path and enhance the surface area of N-GQD/3DrGO. In addition, the interconnected meso‑macroporous structures with major pore size distribution at 2.1 – 77.2 nm is suitable for the acceleration of electron transport at a high scan rate. Moreover, the pseudo-capacitance attributed from the redox reaction of pyrrolic-N/pyridine-N enhances the supercapacitive performance of N-GQD/3DrGO, resulting in the superiority of N-GQD/3DrGO to serve as the electrode material for supercapacitor applications.

The cycling stability is also a crucial index to examine the supercapacitive performance for their practical applications. The long term cyclic stability was tested by using the GCD cycles at 2 A g − 1 for 10,000 cycles in the potential window of 2 V. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the superior cycling stability is mainly due to the highly conductive property of N-GQD/3DrGO-10 network and the support of N-GQDs active sites that tightly connected with 3D rGO structures. The embedment of N-GQDs into the macropores of 3D rGO minimize the loss of N-GQDs activity during the consecutive insertion/exertion of electrolyte ions from the charge-discharge process. Moreover, the well-dispersed N-GQDs enable to prevent the self-restacking of rGO, resulting in the improvement of conductivity and electrochemical stability. It is noted that the mesopore size at 3 – 7 nm can enhance the energy storage capacity of materials, while macroporous architecture in the carbon materials reduces the diffusion length of electrolytes to enhance the stability of rate performance [49,50]. In this study, the continuous pore size distribution of 3.1 – 77.2 nm provides tunable mesopores for energy storage as well as macropores for fast diffusion of electrolyte ions. Therefore, the high capacitance retention of 89.2% can be obtained after 10,000 times of charge-discharge cycles. The GCD curves of N-GQD/3DrGO-10 shows the symmetric shape after long-term cycles. (inset of Fig. 5d). These results evidently indicate the excellent reversibility of charge storage and transport of electroactive N-GQD/3DrGO materials that can support an extremely long lifetime of symmetric supercapacitor device.

In this study, a simple and facile route to fabricate a unique structure containing 0D N-GQDs and 3D rGO as the electrode material for electrochemically supercapacitive application have been developed. The highly ordered 3D network of rGO with open pores possesses high flexibility for the homogeneous embedment of N-GQDs to constitute the N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid nanomaterials. The presence of N-GQDs with uniform small size of 2 – 6 nm inside the 3D network architecture can effectively separate the graphene nanosheets, and change the pore size distribution from 1.8 – 86.8 nm for pure 3D rGO to 2.1 – 77.2 nm for N-GQD/3DrGO, which significantly shortens the ion diffusion path and increases the conductivity of hybrid nanomaterials. Moreover, N-GQDs contain rich functional groups and large amount of edged active sites, which play an important role in boosting the pseudo-capacitive performance. The as-prepared N-GQD/3DrGO hybrid nanomaterial serves as a superior electrode material for symmetric supercapacitor, and the specific capacitance is in the range of 192 – 361 F g − 1 at 2 – 15 A g − 1. In addition, the as-prepared hybrid nanomaterial exhibits a superior energy density of 111.2 − 24.2 Wh kg−1 with a power density of 260 – 5000 W kg−1. Moreover, an excellent cycling stability of N-GQD/3DrGO in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte is achieved, which can maintain 89.2% of the original specific capacitance at 2 A g − 1 after 10,000 cycles. Results clearly indicate that the combination of 0D N-GQDs with 3D rGO is a promising electrode material for supercapacitor applications, which can provide a new direction by fabrication of 3D rGO as the supporting materials for high performance energy storage applications.
